network.overall_strategy: This network targets glutamate through literature-grounded convergent prebiotic routes centered on the hub intermediate alpha-ketoglutarate. Hydrothermal chemistry supplies reduced C1-C3 feedstocks and TCA-like intermediates via Fe0/Fe2+/FeS mineral-promoted CO2 fixation and protometabolic carbon-carbon coupling. Surface chemistry contributes HCN-derived nitrogen chemistry, Strecker-type precursors, cyanosulfidic acrylonitrile chemistry, spark/UV atmospheric organics, and concentration mechanisms. Biochemical/prebiotic stages assemble the final amino acid by transamination, reductive amination, Bucherer-Bergs-type conversion, and hydrolysis of nitrile/polymer precursors. The network explicitly incorporates the key literature result that alpha-ketoglutarate-to-glutamate conversion works in separated steps but fails in one-pot integrated systems, so the pathways emphasize stepwise environmental transfer and compartmentalization.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formate

conditions.other: anoxic; serpentinization-like reducing conditions; based on 30-100C studies

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-mediated reduction of CO2 by H2/Fe0 gives surface-bound reduced carbon that is released as formate.
reactions.reasoning: Native iron reduces CO2 to formate under aqueous prebiotic conditions, providing entry into protometabolism (Varma et al., 2018). FeS/greigite-type minerals also catalyze CO2 reduction to formate in hydrothermal settings (Preiner et al., 2020; Roldan et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal protometabolic formation of pyruvate from reduced C1/C2 feedstocks

conditions.other: anoxic; protometabolic carbon fixation regime; pyruvate observed from CO2 reduction chemistry

reactions.mechanism: Sequential mineral-promoted CO2 reduction and C-C bond formation generate C3 alpha-keto acid products including pyruvate.
reactions.reasoning: Pyruvate is a direct product of Fe0-driven CO2 fixation networks under prebiotic hydrothermal conditions (Varma et al., 2018). Inclusion of magnetite/FeS-type vent minerals is consistent with hydrothermal carbon fixation chemistry described for early protometabolism (Preiner et al., 2020; White et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal/vent-associated glyoxylate generation

conditions.other: anoxic; reduced iron-sulfur surfaces; glyoxylate supplied as protometabolic carbonyl partner

reactions.mechanism: Iron-sulfur surface redox chemistry generates and stabilizes small alpha-oxoacids, including glyoxylate, from reduced carbon pools.
reactions.reasoning: Glyoxylate is a core feedstock in the TCA-like prebiotic network that leads to alpha-ketoglutarate and glutamate (Muchowska et al., 2019; Stubbs et al., 2020). This step is included as a connecting supply reaction; direct geochemical generation is less specifically resolved in the report and is therefore a necessary but partially speculative connector grounded in iron-sulfur hydrothermal carbonyl chemistry (Preiner et al., 2020; Roldan et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Fe2+-promoted TCA-like synthesis of oxaloacetate from pyruvate and glyoxylate

conditions.catalyst: dissolved Fe2+ with Fe0/magnetite mineral context
conditions.other: anoxic; 3 h network timescale; protometabolic TCA-like network

reactions.mechanism: Fe2+-promoted aldol/carboxylation-like transformations couple pyruvate and glyoxylate into higher TCA intermediates including oxaloacetate.
reactions.reasoning: Muchowska et al. demonstrated Fe2+-promoted synthesis of 9 of 11 TCA intermediates, including oxaloacetate, from pyruvate and glyoxylate at 70C (Muchowska et al., 2019). Mineral iron phases are included to satisfy the hydrothermal mineral-agent requirement while remaining faithful to iron-rich vent chemistry.

reactions.name: Fe2+-promoted TCA-like synthesis of alpha-ketoglutarate from pyruvate and glyoxylate

conditions.catalyst: dissolved Fe2+ with Fe0/magnetite mineral context
conditions.other: anoxic; within 3 h TCA-like protometabolic network

reactions.mechanism: Iron-promoted aldol and redox rearrangements extend carbon skeletons from C2/C3 precursors to C5 alpha-ketoacids, giving alpha-ketoglutarate.
reactions.reasoning: Alpha-ketoglutarate was formed in the Fe2+-promoted protometabolic network from pyruvate and glyoxylate at 70C (Muchowska et al., 2019). This is the central biologically relevant precursor to glutamate emphasized in the review.

reactions.name: Metal-free alpha-ketoglutarate formation from pyruvate and glyoxylate

conditions.other: pH 4.5; metal-free; aldol/Cannizzaro-type network

reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate and glyoxylate undergo metal-free aldol additions and Cannizzaro-type redox chemistry to furnish alpha-ketoglutarate and related analogs.
reactions.reasoning: A metal-free pathway from glyoxylate and pyruvate to alpha-ketoglutarate analogs was demonstrated at pH 4.5 and 70C (Stubbs et al., 2020). This provides an independent convergent route to the key hub intermediate.

reactions.name: Hydroxylamine/Fe0 conversion of protometabolic intermediates to glutamate

conditions.other: anoxic; hydroxylamine added to pyruvate/glyoxylate network

reactions.mechanism: Protometabolic generation of alpha-ketoglutarate is followed in situ by nitrogen incorporation under reducing iron conditions to give glutamate among amino acid products.
reactions.reasoning: Muchowska et al. showed that adding hydroxylamine and Fe0 to the pyruvate/glyoxylate TCA-like system generated glutamic acid among four biological amino acids (Muchowska et al., 2019). This is a direct demonstrated route to glutamate.

reactions.name: Transamination of alpha-ketoglutarate with glycine to glutamate

conditions.other: pH 5; 4 h; separated step after alpha-ketoglutarate accumulation

reactions.mechanism: Nonenzymatic transamination transfers the amino group from glycine to alpha-ketoglutarate under alum catalysis, producing glutamate.
reactions.reasoning: Stubbs et al. reported 44% yield of glutamate by transamination of alpha-ketoglutarate with glycine using AlK(SO4)2 at pH 5 and 80C for 4 h (Stubbs et al., 2020). This is one of the highest-yield prebiotic glutamate routes known.

reactions.name: NADH-mediated reductive amination of alpha-ketoglutarate

conditions.other: pH > 7

reactions.mechanism: Alpha-ketoglutarate forms an imine/iminium with ammonia, which is reduced by NADH to glutamate.
reactions.reasoning: Nogal et al. achieved 33% yield of glutamate by NADH-mediated reductive amination of alpha-ketoglutarate at room temperature and pH > 7 (Nogal et al., 2024).

reactions.name: H2-driven nickel-catalyzed reductive amination of alpha-ketoglutarate

conditions.other: pH 8; individual-step system; one-pot integrated network did not accumulate glutamate

reactions.mechanism: Nickel-catalyzed hydrogenative reductive amination converts alpha-ketoglutarate and ammonia to glutamate under mild alkaline conditions.
reactions.reasoning: Kaur et al. demonstrated H2-driven reductive amination of alpha-ketoglutarate to glutamate over nickel catalysts at 22C, pH 8, and 5 bar H2; the same work found glutamate not detectable in the combined one-pot network, highlighting kinetic bottlenecks (Kaur et al., 2024).

reactions.name: Surface pyrite-photocatalyzed reductive amination of alpha-ketoglutarate

conditions.other: strong UV irradiation; anoxic surface pool

reactions.mechanism: Photoexcited mineral surfaces drive electron transfer to imine-like intermediates formed from alpha-ketoglutarate and ammonia, producing glutamate.
reactions.reasoning: The review cites a 2024 Nature Communications study showing UV-irradiated natural pyrite drives reductive amination of alpha-keto acids to amino acids including glutamate. TiO2 is included as a plausible co-photocatalytic surface mineral consistent with UV-active prebiotic surface settings; the core literature support is the pyrite photocatalytic route (Nature Communications, 2024, as summarized in the report).

reactions.name: Surface atmospheric discharge/photolysis generation of HCN-rich feedstocks

conditions.other: spark discharge or UV photolysis in reducing volcanic/sulfurous atmosphere

reactions.mechanism: Atmospheric energy input generates nitriles and reduced nitrogen species that dissolve into surface waters as HCN feedstock.
reactions.reasoning: Miller (1955), Ring et al. (1972), Johnson et al. (2008), Parker et al. (2011), and Sagan & Khare (1971) showed spark or UV processing of reduced gas mixtures generated glutamate and related precursors; HCN-rich atmospheric chemistry is a canonical intermediate feedstock in these systems. This reaction is included as the literature-grounded source of surface HCN for downstream glutamate precursor chemistry.

reactions.name: HCN oligomerization to polymeric nitrile precursors

conditions.other: pH about 9.2; requires HCN concentration above about 0.01 M, achievable by freezing/evaporation

reactions.mechanism: HCN oligomerizes under alkaline concentrated conditions to polymeric nitriles and hetero-organic solids that release amino acids upon hydrolysis.
reactions.reasoning: Ferris et al. showed 0.1 M HCN at pH 9.2 forms oligomers whose hydrolysis releases glutamic acid, and Moser & Matthews detected glutamic acid from hydrolysis of aminoacetonitrile polymers; concentration by freezing was discussed by Sanchez et al. (Ferris et al., 1978; Moser & Matthews, 1968; Sanchez et al., 1966).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of HCN oligomers to glutamate

conditions.other: post-oligomer hydrolysis in concentrated aqueous phase

reactions.mechanism: Polymeric nitriles hydrolyze through amide and acid stages, releasing amino acids including glutamate.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrolysis of HCN oligomers/polymeric nitriles yields glutamic acid among diverse products (Ferris et al., 1978; Moser & Matthews, 1968).

reactions.name: Acrolein-based Strecker precursor formation to glutamate aminonitrile

conditions.other: Michael addition of HCN to acrolein followed by Strecker-type aminonitrile formation

reactions.mechanism: Acrolein undergoes conjugate addition with cyanide, then ammonia/cyanide addition to the carbonyl forms the glutamate-family aminonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: Van Trump & Miller identified acrolein as the key precursor to glutamate in classical Strecker-type chemistry, and Parker et al. confirmed the mechanism in reanalyzed Miller samples; the route proceeds through an aminonitrile intermediate (Van Trump & Miller, 1972; Parker et al., 2011; Miller & Van Trump, 1981).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of glutamate aminonitrile to glutamate

conditions.other: aqueous hydrolysis of nitrile/amide intermediates

reactions.mechanism: The aminonitrile hydrolyzes stepwise through amide/carboxylate stages to glutamate.
reactions.reasoning: The Strecker pathway to glutamate proceeds through an aminonitrile intermediate that hydrolyzes to the amino acid (Van Trump & Miller, 1972; Parker et al., 2011; Miller & Van Trump, 1981).

reactions.name: Surface oxidation of acrolein to succinic semialdehyde

conditions.other: speculative connector; localized oxidant-free carbonyl rearrangement/partial hydration in surface pools

reactions.mechanism: Surface photochemistry and hydration/rearrangement convert allylic aldehyde precursors into the corresponding gamma-aldehydo acid feedstock.
reactions.reasoning: Succinic semialdehyde is identified in the review as the required classical Strecker aldehyde precursor to glutamate, but its prebiotic synthesis remains an open problem explicitly listed as lacking support. This step is therefore included as a clearly speculative but necessary connector to represent the literature-recognized precursor gap (review summary; Van Trump & Miller, 1972; Parker et al., 2011).

reactions.name: Classical Strecker synthesis from succinic semialdehyde to glutamate aminonitrile

conditions.other: aqueous Strecker conditions; dilution-tolerant equilibrium reported

reactions.mechanism: Aldehyde, ammonia, and cyanide form an imine followed by cyanide addition to yield the glutamate-family aminonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: For glutamate, the Strecker route proceeds from succinic semialdehyde or more plausibly acrolein through an aminonitrile intermediate; Miller and Van Trump measured equilibrium constants showing this chemistry can operate at high dilution (Van Trump & Miller, 1972; Miller & Van Trump, 1981; Parker et al., 2011).

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic formation of acrylonitrile from HCN and H2S under UV

conditions.catalyst: UV photoredox on sulfide-rich mineral surfaces
conditions.other: cyanosulfidic network

reactions.mechanism: UV-driven cyanosulfidic chemistry converts nitrile feedstocks into activated unsaturated nitriles such as acrylonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. established prebiotic cyanosulfidic chemistry from HCN and H2S under photoredox conditions, and Kuroda & Kobayashi highlighted acrylonitrile as a glutamate/glutamine precursor in this network (Patel et al., 2015; Kuroda & Kobayashi, 2024).

reactions.name: Acrylonitrile conjugate-addition route to glutamate aminonitrile precursor

conditions.other: conjugate addition and nitrile functionalization in concentrated surface pools

reactions.mechanism: Acrylonitrile acts as an activated C3 nitrile acceptor that, after conjugate addition and nitrogen incorporation, yields a glutamate-family nitrile precursor.
reactions.reasoning: The review states that acrylonitrile generated in cyanosulfidic chemistry undergoes conjugate addition to afford glutamine or glutamic acid precursors (Kuroda & Kobayashi, 2024), building on the cyanosulfidic network of Patel et al. (2015). This reaction is included as the corresponding precursor-forming step.

reactions.name: Bucherer-Bergs-type conversion of alpha-ketoglutarate to glutamate

conditions.other: via hydantoin intermediate; stepwise conversion

reactions.mechanism: Alpha-ketoglutarate reacts with cyanide and ammonia through a hydantoin intermediate that hydrolyzes to glutamate.
reactions.reasoning: The review identifies the Bucherer-Bergs pathway as directly applicable to alpha-ketoglutarate to glutamic acid through hydantoin intermediates (Pulletikurti et al., 2022).

reactions.name: FeS-mediated reduction of NO to ammonia

conditions.other: anoxic; mild conditions

reactions.mechanism: Iron sulfide mediates sequential reduction of nitric oxide to ammonia, supplying fixed nitrogen for aminations and Strecker chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Summers & Chang demonstrated that FeS reduces NO to NH3 under mild conditions, addressing the nitrogen incorporation bottleneck noted in the review (Summers & Chang, 1993). This step connects atmospheric/surface nitrogen chemistry to hydrothermal amination routes.

reactions.name: Glutamate cyclization to pyroglutamate

conditions.other: thermal dehydration/cyclization; degradation sink

reactions.mechanism: Intramolecular cyclization of glutamate forms 5-oxoproline, reducing free glutamate availability.
reactions.reasoning: The review identifies cyclization of glutamic acid to pyroglutamic acid as a glutamate-specific stability problem at elevated temperatures, explaining low accumulation in some hydrothermal simulations (Bada et al., 1995; Pérez-Villa et al., 2018; review summary).

pathways.name: Fe2+-promoted protometabolic network plus hydroxylamine
pathways.description: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction and TCA-like chemistry generate glutamate directly when hydroxylamine and Fe0 are added to the pyruvate/glyoxylate network.

pathways.name: Fe2+-promoted alpha-ketoglutarate then alum-catalyzed transamination
pathways.description: Hydrothermal formation of alpha-ketoglutarate is followed by separate-step transamination with glycine, matching the highest-yield demonstrated route.

pathways.name: Fe2+-promoted alpha-ketoglutarate then NADH reductive amination
pathways.description: Hydrothermal protometabolism generates alpha-ketoglutarate, which is aminated in a mild room-temperature biochemical setting using NADH.

pathways.name: Fe2+-promoted alpha-ketoglutarate then nickel/H2 reductive amination
pathways.description: Hydrothermal alpha-ketoglutarate is transferred into a separated H2-driven nickel-catalyzed reductive amination environment.

pathways.name: Fe2+-promoted alpha-ketoglutarate then pyrite photocatalysis
pathways.description: Hydrothermal synthesis of alpha-ketoglutarate is followed by UV-driven surface reductive amination on pyrite.

pathways.name: Metal-free alpha-ketoglutarate then transamination
pathways.description: Pyruvate and glyoxylate generate alpha-ketoglutarate in a metal-free biochemical network, then glycine transamination yields glutamate.

pathways.name: HCN oligomerization and hydrolysis
pathways.description: Surface-concentrated HCN oligomerizes and hydrolyzes to release glutamate directly.

pathways.name: Acrolein-based Strecker route
pathways.description: Atmospheric/surface chemistry supplies HCN; acrolein undergoes Michael-Strecker chemistry to glutamate aminonitrile, which hydrolyzes to glutamate.

pathways.name: Succinic semialdehyde classical Strecker route
pathways.description: A literature-recognized but experimentally unresolved succinic semialdehyde precursor feeds a classical Strecker aminonitrile route to glutamate.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic acrylonitrile route
pathways.description: HCN and H2S photochemistry yields acrylonitrile, which furnishes glutamate-family nitrile precursors that hydrolyze to glutamate.
